slides.title:
Home Physical
Therapy Platform
slides.description: WizeCare lets physiotherapists deliver treatment remotely. The clinical tools were there — but the platform was too complex to use under real clinical pressure.

items.label: Role
items.value: UX/UI Designer — Research, Workflow Design, Interaction Design, Prototyping

slides.label: The Context
slides.title: Built for clinicians who don't have time to learn software
slides.content: WizeCare is used by physiotherapists managing patients remotely — under time pressure, with low tolerance for tools that slow them down.
slides.highlight: For WizeCare to succeed, the system needed to feel like a time-saver — not an extra task.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: The platform had the right tools. The experience let them down.
slides.description: Despite strong clinical functionality, adoption was low. The same three friction points came up across every user we spoke to.

issues.title: Technology hesitation
issues.description: Digital workflows felt unfamiliar compared to manual routines

issues.title: Too much at once
issues.description: Dense screens with no hierarchy left clinicians unsure where to start

issues.title: Fragmented workflows
issues.description: Core tasks spread across multiple screens with no clear thread

slides.label: User Research
slides.title: What clinicians told us
slides.content: We visited clinics, ran interviews, and reviewed support tickets. The same frustrations came up again and again.

quotes.text: I spend more time navigating than managing my patients.
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, Clinic A

quotes.text: I never know which screen has what I need.
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, Clinic B

quotes.text: Editing a care plan takes so many steps — I avoid it when I can.
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, Clinic C

quotes.text: I still use paper notes because they're faster.
quotes.author: Physiotherapist, Clinic D

goals.title: Cut the cognitive load
goals.description: Show only what clinicians need — nothing more.

goals.title: Make core actions obvious
goals.description: No hunting, no guessing — common tasks immediately reachable.

goals.title: Design for both ends of expertise
goals.description: New users need guidance. Experienced users need speed.

goals.title: Reduce reliance on support
goals.description: Self-explanatory enough that clinicians don't need to ask for help.

slides.title: Patient Management
slides.subtitle: The first place clinicians land — and where they spent most of their time feeling lost.

slides.label: Patient Management
slides.title: Finding patient information shouldn't be a search task
slides.beforeDescription: Patient details, care plans, and adherence metrics were scattered with no clear structure.
slides.afterDescription: A single patient page brings everything together with a clear, predictable hierarchy.
beforeBullets: Data split across multiple screens with no clear ownership
Adherence metrics buried — hardest to find, most clinically useful
afterBullets: All patient information centralised in one view
Clear separation between info, plans, and progress
Adherence metrics surfaced as the primary clinical signal
slides.highlight: Clinicians went from navigating between screens to finding everything in one place.

slides.title: Care Plan Editing
slides.subtitle: The most frequent task in the platform — and the one with the most unnecessary steps.

slides.label: Care Plan Editing
slides.title: Editing a plan shouldn't feel like a multi-screen journey
slides.beforeDescription: Editing a care plan meant navigating 4–5 screens, losing context at every step.
slides.afterDescription: The entire editing flow now lives in one focused screen — inline, contextual, and fast.
beforeBullets: 4–5 screens to complete a single edit
Context lost every time a new screen loaded
Exercises in dense lists — easy to misread, hard to reorder
afterBullets: Single screen for the full editing flow
Inline editing — no modals, no page changes
slides.highlight: Patient health data pinned throughout — always visible while editing.

slides.title: Protocol Builder
slides.subtitle: Experienced physiotherapists build their own protocols — the old system gave them no way to do that.

slides.label: Protocol Builder
slides.title: Expert users need a system that keeps up with them
slides.beforeDescription: No real protocol creation flow. Expert clinicians were forced into rigid templates that didn't match how they work.
slides.afterDescription: A Kanban-style builder gives expert users full control — structured the way physiotherapists naturally think.
beforeBullets: Rigid templates didn't fit complex cases
No way to reorder or group exercises
afterBullets: Exercise library left, treatment board right
Same card system as care plan editor — familiar from day one
slides.highlight: Reusing the card system across flows meant zero learning curve for the new builder.

slides.label: Outcomes
slides.title: Less friction. More time for patients.

outcomes.title: 10–15 min saved per patient
outcomes.description: Streamlined workflows meant less time navigating, more time on clinical decisions.

outcomes.title: Fewer errors
outcomes.description:
Clearer layouts and inline editing reduced misclicks 
and prescription mistakes.

outcomes.title: Daily usage increased
outcomes.description: Clinicians who avoided the platform started using it as their primary tool.

outcomes.title: Support requests dropped
outcomes.description: A self-explanatory system meant less dependence on training.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: Designing systems that respect the people who use them.
